title: Polysemy IS Operator Superposition
subtitle: Why 57% of 'Polysemanticity' Is Actually Schankian Operator Structure

props.title: The Revelation: Polysemy Was Never Noise

children.children: In April 2026, Ait Hou et al. dropped a bomb on the mechanistic interpretability community: 57% of what standard metrics call 'polysemanticity' in neural networks is actually just polysemy — the same word having multiple meanings. The interpretability community treated this as a confound to correct. We think they have it exactly backwards.

Polysemy isn't noise contaminating your superposition measurements. Polysemy IS the phenomenon — it's what happens when a single lexeme maps to different Schankian primitive operators depending on context. The neuron that fires for both 'bank' (financial) and 'bank' (riverside) isn't confused — it's detecting a shared abstract property that our operator framework can identify.

props.source: Imagine a prism splitting white light into its component colors. SAE decomposition is that prism — and the 'colors' it reveals are Schankian primitives superposed in the same neural activation.
props.target: A polysemantic neuron isn't broken or inefficient. It's a compressed representation of multiple operators sharing the same computational substrate, exactly as superposition theory predicts — but with structure that polysemy makes legible.

children.children: Core Claim: There are three distinct phenomena hiding under 'polysemanticity': (1) True superposition — unrelated features sharing dimensions under capacity pressure; (2) Operator polysemy — same lexeme activating different primitive compositions per sense; (3) Operator composition — compound operators superposed at the same token. Schankian primitives are the ground truth that separates them.

Elhage et al. (2022) proposed that neural networks represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding them in superposition — overlapping, nearly-orthogonal directions in activation space. This explained polysemanticity (neurons responding to seemingly unrelated inputs) as an efficient packing strategy.

items.definition: Encoding more features than dimensions by using nearly-orthogonal directions that interfere slightly but remain decodable.
A neuron that activates for multiple, apparently unrelated concepts — the observable symptom of superposition.
A linguistic phenomenon where one word form has multiple related meanings (e.g., 'run' a race vs 'run' a company).
A dictionary learning method that decomposes superposed activations into individual monosemantic features.

children.children: The key innovation of SAEs (Bricken et al. 2023) was showing you could decompose these superposed representations into interpretable features. But nobody asked: what IS the structure that polysemy contributes? Is it just noise, or does it encode something meaningful?
We propose that what looks like a single phenomenon — a neuron firing for multiple things — is actually three completely different mechanisms operating simultaneously:

props.title: Decomposing Polysemanticity
props.description: Click each type to see how it manifests in neural activations and how Schankian operators help distinguish them.

tabs.label: Type 1: True Superposition
tabs.content:
**What it is:** Unrelated features packed into the same dimensions due to capacity pressure. Example: A neuron responds to both 'quantum' (physics) and 'tulip' (botany) — no semantic connection, just efficient packing.

**How to detect:** After conditioning on Schankian operator labels, this residual randomness remains. No operator structure explains the co-activation.

**What SAEs do:** Cleanly separate these into distinct dictionary elements with no shared operator signature.

tabs.label: Type 2: Operator Polysemy
tabs.content:
**What it is:** Same word maps to different primitive compositions per sense. Example: 'Run' activates PTRANS (physical movement: 'run home'), PROPEL (machine operation: 'run the engine'), or ATRANS (organizational control: 'run the company').

**How to detect:** The neuron's activation pattern clusters by operator label, not by surface word. Context determines which primitive fires.

**What SAEs do:** Decompose into features aligned with each primitive sense. The number of SAE components for a polysemous verb should equal its operator decomposition count.

tabs.label: Type 3: Operator Composition
tabs.content:
**What it is:** Compound operators superposed at the same token position. Example: 'Sell' simultaneously encodes ATRANS(object: seller→buyer) AND ATRANS(money: buyer→seller) — two primitive operations happening at once.

**How to detect:** SAE decomposition reveals multiple primitive-aligned features at a single token. The composition structure (which primitives combine) is predictable from the verb's semantics.

**What SAEs do:** Reveal the component primitives of compound operators. Vector arithmetic should work: vec('trade') ≈ vec(ATRANS↑) + vec(ATRANS↓).

props.title: The Ait Hou Result: 57% Is Structure

children.children: Ait Hou et al. designed a clever experiment: they created conditions where the same word appeared with different meanings (lexical-only) versus where different words shared the same meaning (semantic-only). Their key finding: the lexical-only condition consistently produced higher polysemanticity scores than the semantic-only condition.

props.title: The 'Bank' Experiment
props.code:
# Lexical-only condition (same word, different meanings):
# 'bank' (financial institution) → neuron fires
# 'bank' (riverside) → SAME neuron fires
# → Metrics say: polysemantic!

# Semantic-only condition (different words, same meaning):
# 'lender' (financial) → neuron fires  
# 'creditor' (financial) → SAME neuron fires
# → Metrics say: monosemantic (both are financial)

# The 57% confound:
# Most 'polysemanticity' comes from lexical identity,
# not from unrelated features sharing dimensions.

# OUR reframe:
# 'bank' (financial) → ATRANS operator (money transfer)
# 'bank' (riverside) → LOCALE feature (spatial reference)
# The neuron ISN'T confused — it's detecting a shared
# abstract property OR is genuinely superposed.
props.explanation: The paper treats polysemy as noise. We treat it as signal — evidence that the network has learned operator-level decompositions that happen to share a lexical form.

props.title: Why the interpretability community got this backwards
props.content: The superposition community wants monosemantic features. Finding that 57% of polysemanticity is 'just polysemy' seems like good news — correct for it and your SAEs get cleaner. But from the computational semantics perspective, polysemy is the most interesting part! It reveals that the network has learned to decompose meaning into primitives that cross-cut lexical boundaries. A polysemous verb like 'run' doesn't have 7 unrelated meanings — it has 7 different operator compositions that share a morphological form for historical reasons. The network's job is EXACTLY to resolve which operator applies in context. That's not a bug — it's the core computation.

children.children: Here's where our operator discovery work (arXiv:2604.04756) provides the missing piece. We've shown that wake-sleep compression rediscovers Schank's conceptual primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, INGEST, etc.) from data. These primitives give us labeled ground truth for what polysemous words actually decompose into.

props.title: Polysemous Verb → Operator Decomposition
props.description: See how common polysemous verbs decompose into different Schankian primitives per sense. Each sense is a distinct operator composition.

children.children: The key prediction: SAE features for polysemous verbs should cluster by operator type, not by surface form. All PTRANS senses (run physically, flow, drive) should share SAE features, regardless of the verb used. And compound verbs (sell, trade, teach) should show multiple primitive features simultaneously.
If compound operators truly superpose (rather than compose sequentially), we get a powerful testable prediction: operator vector arithmetic should work for novel compounds.

props.title: Compound Operator Arithmetic
props.code:
# If SAE features decompose operators cleanly:
vec('sell') ≈ vec(ATRANS_forward) + vec(ATRANS_backward)
vec('trade') ≈ vec(ATRANS_forward) + vec(ATRANS_backward)  # similar!
vec('steal') ≈ vec(ATRANS_forward) + vec(NOT_SANCTIONED)

# Novel prediction (never seen in training):
vec('barter') ≈ vec(ATRANS_forward) + vec(ATRANS_backward) + vec(NO_MONEY)

# Cross-primitive composition:
vec('mail') ≈ vec(ATRANS) + vec(PTRANS)  # transfer + physical movement
vec('email') ≈ vec(ATRANS) + vec(MTRANS) # transfer + info movement
vec('mail') - vec('email') ≈ vec(PTRANS) - vec(MTRANS)  # physical vs info!
props.explanation: This is analogous to word2vec's famous 'king - man + woman = queen' but at the OPERATOR level. If the decomposition is real, operator arithmetic should be more consistent than word-level arithmetic because it operates on genuine semantic primitives rather than surface distributional statistics.

props.title: Why operator arithmetic should work better than word arithmetic
props.content: Word2vec arithmetic works inconsistently because words are surface forms entangling multiple semantic dimensions. 'King - man + woman = queen' works because gender happens to be cleanly encoded, but 'doctor - man + woman' doesn't reliably give 'nurse' because the profession and gender dimensions aren't orthogonal at the word level. Operator arithmetic should be MORE consistent because: (1) primitives are genuinely orthogonal semantic dimensions (ATRANS vs PTRANS capture independent aspects of meaning); (2) SAEs are explicitly trained to find these independent directions; (3) composition structure is regular (all exchange verbs compose the same way). This predicts that SAE-space operator arithmetic will have higher consistency than embedding-space word arithmetic.

children.children: The most direct test: replicate Ait Hou's methodology but ADD operator labels. Their 57% confound ratio should collapse when you condition on which Schankian primitive is active in context.

props.title: Predicted Confound Ratio Collapse
props.description: The 57% 'confound' from polysemy should be explained (reduced to near-zero) once we condition on operator labels. Each step shows what happens as we add more semantic structure.

steps.label: Baseline (Ait Hou)
steps.description: 57% of polysemanticity attributed to lexical identity (polysemy). Treated as confound.

steps.label: + Operator Labels
steps.description: Condition each activation on its Schankian operator. Polysemous verbs split into operator-specific clusters. Predicted confound drop: 57% → ~15%.

steps.label: + Composition Structure
steps.description: Account for compound operators (sell = ATRANS+ATRANS). Remaining multi-feature activations explained by composition. Predicted: ~15% → ~5%.

steps.label: True Residual
steps.description: Remaining ~5% is genuine superposition — unrelated features sharing dimensions due to capacity pressure. THIS is the real confound; the 57% was always signal.

children.children: This reframing connects to several threads in our research program:

items.definition: Our finding of a 'case system' in SAE space (nominative/accusative features) — these ARE operator role-marking directions being superposed with content features.

Frequency bias determines which primitives get dedicated dimensions vs which are forced into superposition. Rare primitives (emotion, mental construction) are superposed; common ones (ATRANS) get their own directions.
Superposition IS type erasure at the vector level. Coarser SAE dictionaries erase type parameters; finer ones recover them. The 86% sparsity in type×domain matrices is superposition geometry.
The MLP exception handler's job is resolving WHICH superposed primitive to activate in context. It's a disambiguation engine for operator polysemy.
MLP consensus neurons detect the dominant primitive; exception neurons fire when superposed primitives compete. The architecture IS a polysemy resolver.

children.children: The mechanistic interpretability community (Anthropic, EleutherAI) and the computational semantics community (Schank, Jackendoff, FrameNet) have been studying the same phenomenon from opposite directions without realizing it:

props.title: The Same Problem, Two Vocabularies
props.description: Map between interpretability language and computational semantics language. They describe identical structures.

left.label: Interpretability Community
items: Superposition → features packed into shared dimensions
Polysemanticity → neurons fire for multiple concepts
SAE features → monosemantic directions in activation space
Dictionary learning → finding the 'true' feature set
Feature splitting → finer decomposition at larger dictionaries

right.label: Computational Semantics
items: Semantic composition → primitives combine for complex meanings
Polysemy → words map to multiple primitive structures
Operator discovery → finding the minimal event vocabulary

children.children: The bridge insight: SAE dictionary learning IS operator discovery. Both seek the minimal set of atomic elements from which all observed patterns can be composed. The interpretability community does it bottom-up (from activations); we do it top-down (from event descriptions). They should converge — and our ATOMIC/GLUCOSE experiments show they do.

questions.question: According to Ait Hou et al. (2026), what percentage of measured 'polysemanticity' is actually due to polysemy (lexical identity)?
questions.explanation: Their study found that 57% of what standard metrics flag as polysemanticity comes from the same word having multiple meanings, not from unrelated features sharing dimensions.

questions.question: In our framework, what distinguishes 'operator polysemy' from 'true superposition'?
options: Operator polysemy involves related meanings; true superposition involves unrelated features
They are identical phenomena with different names
True superposition only occurs in larger models
questions.explanation: Operator polysemy has structure: the same word maps to different but specific primitive compositions depending on context. True superposition is the residual — genuinely unrelated features packed together under capacity pressure with no semantic relationship.

questions.question: Why should operator arithmetic (in SAE space) work more consistently than word2vec arithmetic?
options: Operators are genuinely orthogonal semantic dimensions, while words entangle multiple dimensions
Word2vec uses a smaller training corpus
questions.explanation: Schankian primitives capture independent semantic dimensions (ATRANS vs PTRANS are genuinely different operations), while words entangle many dimensions simultaneously. SAEs are trained to find exactly these independent directions, making arithmetic more regular.

questions.question: What does 'sell' decompose into in Schankian operator space?
options: ATRANS(goods, seller→buyer) composed with ATRANS(money, buyer→seller)
questions.explanation: 'Sell' is a compound operator: it's two ATRANSes (abstract transfers) happening simultaneously — goods move from seller to buyer, AND money moves from buyer to seller. In the neural representation, these two primitives are SUPERPOSED at the 'sell' token position.

questions.question: What should happen to the 57% 'confound ratio' when we condition on Schankian operator labels?
options: It should increase because operators add complexity
It should stay the same because polysemy is irreducible
It should collapse toward zero because the polysemy is explained by operator structure
questions.explanation: If polysemy is genuinely operator superposition (not random noise), then labeling which operator is active in each context should explain the multi-activation pattern. The 'confound' disappears because it was always meaningful structure, not measurement error.

children.children:
• Ait Hou et al. 2026 — 'Polysemanticity or Polysemy? Lexical Identity Confounds Superposition Metrics' (arXiv:2604.00443)
• Elhage et al. 2022 — 'Toy Models of Superposition' (Anthropic)
• Bricken et al. 2023 — 'Towards Monosemanticity: Decomposing Language Models With Dictionary Learning' (Anthropic)
• Lecomte et al. 2025 — 'Polysemanticity without capacity pressure'
• Balogh 2026 — 'The Discrete Charm of the MLP' (arXiv:2603.10985)
• Balogh 2026 — 'Darkness Visible' (arXiv:2604.04756)
• Schank 1975 — 'Conceptual Information Processing' (original primitive operators)
